topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of the coffee shop felt like a physical weight pressing down on my chest. I stared at the latte art—a delicate swirl of foam—as if it held the answer to why Leo had simply… erased me. He was laughing now, a bright, easy sound directed at the woman across the table, and I could feel a cold, sharp ache bloom beneath my ribs. Every shared memory, every inside joke, felt like a carefully constructed illusion that he had just carelessly discarded. I watched his hand gesture animatedly, and a strange, brittle tension tightened my jaw. I wanted to lash out, to shatter the placid scene, but my hands remained still, clenched tight in my lap. It was a profound sense of being unseen, a vacuum where my significance used to reside. When he finally turned, his eyes swept the room, and for a fleeting second, they didn't land on me. That momentary blankness was a punch to the gut. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of wood against the floor sounding deafening in the sudden quiet of my own misery.

[story 2]

I traced the outline of his name on my phone screen, the letters blurring under the sheen of my unshed tears. It was absurd. After all those years, after sharing scraped knees and whispered secrets under the stars, he couldn't even recall my face. The bitterness rose in my throat, thick and metallic. I stood by the window, watching the city lights smear across the wet pavement, feeling utterly separate from the warmth emanating from the bar inside. A slow, deliberate movement pulled my coat tighter around me. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to force the churning nausea down. When he finally approached, his expression was one of genuine warmth, the kind reserved for casual acquaintances. He offered a friendly nod, a polite, distant acknowledgment. I met his gaze, and instead of the familiar spark, there was only a flat, unreadable surface. I felt a profound disconnect, a sickening realization that the foundation of our shared history was entirely one-sided. I turned away before I could voice the sharp, ugly questions that clawed at the edges of my composure.

[story 3]

The memory was a jagged shard, catching the light just right. I replayed the last conversation, the casual way he’d dismissed my shared childhood adventures as 'just stuff kids do.' It wasn't just forgetting; it was an active erasure. A deep, corrosive feeling settled in my core, heavy and suffocating. I watched him across the crowded room, his easy camaraderie with everyone else a stark contrast to the isolating void I inhabited. My knuckles were white where I gripped my glass. I felt a fierce, hot surge of resentment, a burning need to point out the obvious, to force him to acknowledge the bond we once shared. But the words caught in my throat, strangled by a sudden, desperate need to keep the pain contained, to let the sheer weight of the betrayal settle into a cold, immovable stone in my stomach. I simply watched him, a statue carved from simmering disappointment.
